content_en:
Muruj al-Dhahab is organized around a series of broad themes that al-Mas'udi uses to structure his encyclopedic material. The opening sections on geography and cosmology are followed by accounts of ancient peoples: the Babylonians, Assyrians, Persians, Greeks, Romans, Indians, Chinese, and Africans. Al-Mas'udi draws on both written sources and oral traditions gathered in his travels, presenting a picture of world history remarkable in its geographic breadth for a tenth-century author.

The sections on pre-Islamic Arabia are particularly valuable as historical sources. Al-Mas'udi records traditions about the ancient Arab kingdoms of Saba, Himyar, and Kinda, the tribal structures of the Hijaz, and the religious landscape of Arabia before Islam. These accounts, while not always historically verifiable, preserve traditions that complement or supplement other early Arabic sources and that illuminate the world into which the Prophet Muhammad was born.

The treatment of the Islamic period focuses heavily on the caliphate. Al-Mas'udi provides detailed accounts of each caliph, drawn from a wide range of sources, including oral traditions from people he met in his travels who claimed family or personal connections to the events they described. His account of the first four caliphs, the Umayyad period, and the early Abbasids is a rich source of narrative traditions, though not primarily a work of critical historical scholarship.

One of the distinctive features of Muruj al-Dhahab is its treatment of the non-Arab peoples of the Islamic world. Al-Mas'udi gives extensive attention to the Persians, Turks, Berbers, and Indian peoples, documenting their histories, social structures, and religious practices with a sympathetic curiosity unusual in classical Arabic literature. This cross-cultural dimension makes the work particularly valuable for historians of the non-Arab Islamic world. Al-Mas'udi's account of the Turks, written before Turkic peoples became the dominant political force in the central Islamic lands, is particularly notable as early evidence from an informed observer about the societies that would transform the Islamic world in the eleventh and twelfth centuries. His observations on the Buwayhid and Hamdanid dynasties, written near the end of his life when these powers were reshaping the political map, add a contemporary dimension to the historical reach of the work.
content_id:
Muruj al-Dhahab diorganisasikan di sekitar serangkaian tema-tema luas yang al-Mas'udi kembalikan berulang kali sepanjang teks. Di antara tema-tema ini adalah: hubungan antara geografi dan sejarah, keragaman budaya dan agama di dunia Islam dan sekitarnya, sifat kekuasaan dan keadilan politik, dan akumulasi pengetahuan melalui perjalanan dan observasi. Tema-tema ini menyatukan apa yang sebaliknya mungkin tampak sebagai kumpulan penggalan yang tidak terhubung.

Cakupan sejarah Islam dalam Muruj al-Dhahab sangat berharga. Al-Mas'udi mendokumentasikan era Nabi ﷺ dan Khulafa' ar-Rasyidin, Dinasti Umayyah, dan era Abbasiyah awal dan tengah dengan penggambaran yang sering menyertakan detail yang tidak tersedia dari sumber-sumber lain. Beliau mewawancarai orang-orang yang memiliki kenangan keluarga tentang peristiwa-peristiwa seabad lalu, beliau mengakses arsip-arsip yang tidak lagi tersedia, dan beliau mengintegrasikan informasi dari sumber-sumber berbahasa Persia, Yunani, dan lainnya ke dalam karyanya.

Cakupan geografi al-Mas'udi adalah di antara yang paling komprehensif yang dihasilkan oleh penulis mana pun sebelum era modern. Beliau mendiskusikan Laut Mediterania, Samudra Hindia, pantai Afrika Timur, Laut Kaspia, dan pedalaman Asia dengan tingkat detail yang mengagumkan. Rincian etnografisnya tentang berbagai orang — Berber, Turki, India, Africans, Eropa — memberikan gambaran tentang dunia yang dikenal oleh sarjana Muslim abad ke-10 dengan cara yang tidak ada karya lain dari periode tersebut yang benar-benar cocok.

Satu tema yang menonjol dalam Muruj al-Dhahab adalah minat al-Mas'udi terhadap perbandingan agama dan budaya. Beliau mendiskusikan Kristen, Zoroastrianisme, Hinduisme, dan sistem kepercayaan lainnya dengan minat yang tulus dan relatif bebas dari polemik yang lebih biasa dalam tulisan Muslim abad pertengahan tentang agama-agama lain. Ini bukan berarti beliau netral — beliau Muslim dan menulis dari perspektif Muslim — tetapi rasa ingin tahunya tentang budaya dan kepercayaan lain menghasilkan penggambaran yang jauh lebih informatif daripada yang biasanya dihasilkan oleh penulis yang lebih apologetis.

Cakupan ilmu pengetahuan dan filsafat dalam Muruj al-Dhahab mencerminkan keterlibatan intelektual luas al-Mas'udi. Beliau mendiskusikan ilmu astronomi, filsafat Yunani (terutama Aristoteles dan Plato), dan tradisi-tradisi ilmiah dari berbagai peradaban dengan cara yang menunjukkan pemahaman yang nyata tentang materi. Keterlibatan al-Mas'udi dengan filsafat Yunani dan ilmu-ilmu Islam mencerminkan lingkungan intelektual Baghdad abad ke-10 yang lebih luas di mana tradisi-tradisi yang diterjemahkan ini aktif diasimilasi dan didiskusikan.
content_ar: Muruj al-Dhahab is organized around a series of broad themes that al-Mas'udi uses to structure his encyclopedic material. The opening sections on geography and cosmology are followed by accounts of ancient peoples: the Babylonians, Assyrians, Persians, Greeks, Romans, Indians, Chinese, and Africans. Al-Mas'udi draws on both written sources and oral traditions gathered in his travels, presenting a picture of الدنيا history remarkable in its geographic breadth for a عاشراً-century author. The sections on pre-Islamic Arabia are particularly valuable as historical sources. Al-Mas'udi records traditions about the ancient Arab kingdoms of Saba, Himyar, and Kinda, the tribal structures of the Hijaz, and the religious landscape of Arabia قبل الإسلام. These accounts, بينما not always historically verifiable, preserve traditions that complement or supplement other early Arabic sources and that illuminate the الدنيا into which النبي ﷺ محمد ﷺ was born. The treatment of the Islamic period focuses heavily on the الخلافة. Al-Mas'udi provides detailed accounts of each caliph, drawn from a wide range of sources, بما في ذلك oral traditions from people he met in his travels who claimed الأسرة or personal connections to the events they described. His account of the أولاً four caliphs, the Umayyad period, and the early Abbasids is a rich source of narrative traditions, though not primarily a work of البالغ الأهمية historical scholarship. One of the distinctive features of Muruj al-Dhahab is its treatment of the non-Arab peoples of the Islamic الدنيا. Al-Mas'udi gives extensive attention to the Persians, Turks, Berbers, and Indian peoples, documenting their histories, social structures, and religious practices with a sympathetic curiosity unusual in الكلاسيكي Arabic literature. This cross-cultural dimension makes the work particularly valuable for historians of the non-Arab Islamic الدنيا. Al-Mas'udi's account of the Turks, written قبل Turkic peoples became the dominant political force in the المحوري Islamic lands, is particularly notable as early evidence from an informed observer about the societies that would transform the Islamic الدنيا in the eleventh and twelfth centuries. His observations on the Buwayhid and Hamdanid dynasties, written near the end of his الحياة عندما these powers were reshaping the political map, add a contemporary dimension to the historical reach of the work.
